title: Banana bread, classic
subtitle: The quick loaf made for ripe bananas
excerpt: A one-bowl banana loaf with a moist, tight crumb and a cracked brown crust. The riper the bananas, the better the loaf: the sugars concentrate as the skins blacken.
sourceNotes: Banana quick breads are an American interwar development, appearing in baking powder manufacturer cookbooks from the 1930s as a way to use overripe fruit. The pattern of mashed banana, fat, egg, and baking powder in a loaf tin is widely published in USDA and extension service bulletins from that era. This version follows the standard British domestic treatment, using plain flour and bicarbonate of soda rather than self-raising.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze flat. Toast from frozen or defrost at room temperature.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles to two tins. Use the same oven on different shelves; swap the tins halfway.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The loaf keeps well for 3 days wrapped at room temperature, improving slightly on the second day.

content.text: The best banana bread starts with bananas you have been meaning to throw away. Black-skinned, overripe bananas that smell intensely sweet are correct for this loaf. Yellow bananas with no spots produce a much blander result. If you have ripe bananas but are not ready to bake, peel them, seal in a bag, and freeze them: defrost in a bowl over a plate, as they release a lot of liquid.

items.prepNote: large, peeled and mashed (about 300 g peeled weight)

content.text: Heat the oven to 175°C fan. Grease a 900 g / 2 lb loaf tin and line the base with a strip of baking paper. Mash the bananas well in a large bowl; a few lumps are fine but the texture should mostly be smooth.

Add the melted butter, sugar, eggs, and vanilla to the banana and stir until combined. Sift over the flour, bicarbonate of soda, and salt. Fold in with a spatula using a figure-of-eight motion until no dry flour remains. Do not beat: overworking the batter develops gluten and makes the crumb tough. A few strokes past 'just combined' is fine; thirty extra strokes is not.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin and level the surface. Bake for 50 to 55 minutes. The loaf is done when it is deep golden brown, a skewer through the centre comes out clean (a few moist crumbs are fine; wet batter is not), and the sides have begun to pull away from the tin. Domestic ovens vary; check at 45 minutes and cover loosely with foil if the top is darkening faster than the centre is setting. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack.
